ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general squid food product class; no specific entry for Vietnamese stir-fried squid with cucumber and tomato. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers squid/cuttlefish products; no specific code for this Vietnamese composite dish. Closest parent match used; composite dish with vegetables not individually coded.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of stir-fried squid with cucumber and tomato is approximately 350 g, typical of a Vietnamese restaurant or hawker portion.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Squid, mixed species, raw (FDC ID 175168) combined with component estimates for cucumber, tomato, fish sauce, and soy sauce
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Vietnamese stir-fried squid with cucumber and tomato exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values were estimated using USDA FoodData Central data for raw squid (FDC ID 175168) as the primary protein/fat/cholesterol anchor, supplemented with USDA data for cucumber (FDC ID 168409), tomato (FDC ID 170457), fish sauce (FDC ID 173580), and soy sauce (FDC ID 172238). The input record's per_100g values (energy 108.57 kcal, protein 10.0 g, fat 5.14 g, carbohydrate 5.14 g) are broadly consistent with a mixed squid-vegetable stir-fry and have been retained with minor rounding, as they fall within the plausible range for this dish type. Sodium 342.86 mg/100g is consistent with fish sauce and soy sauce seasoning. Cholesterol 128.57 mg/100g is consistent with squid. Values adopted from input record and cross-validated against USDA component data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on mixed seafood stir-fry with low-GI vegetables
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese stir-fried squid with cucumber and tomato. GI estimated at approximately 35 (low) based on: (1) squid has negligible carbohydrate and does not contribute to GI; (2) cucumber GI ~15 (University of Sydney GI Database); (3) tomato GI ~15 (University of Sydney GI Database); (4) the dish's total carbohydrate is 5.1 g/100g, predominantly from vegetables with low intrinsic GI; (5) protein (10 g/100g) and fat (5.1 g/100g) slow gastric emptying, further attenuating postprandial glucose response. Composite GI estimated at 35 (low). Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (35 × 5.1 × 3.5) / 100 = 6.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 342.9 mg/100g from fish sauce and soy sauce; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,200 mg sodium, representing 52–60% of the recommended daily limit of 2,000–2,300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and soy sauce, or use low-sodium alternatives, to keep a single serving below 600 mg sodium; increasing the proportion of cucumber and tomato will also improve potassium offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (5.1 g/100g) and estimated low GI (~35) result in a glycemic load of approximately 6.3 per 350 g serving, which is well within safe limits for blood glucose management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate load and high protein content (10 g/100g) from squid, which moderates postprandial glucose response; monitor cumulative sodium from seasoning and limit added sugar in the sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content is high at 128.6 mg/100g from squid; a 350 g serving provides approximately 450 mg dietary cholesterol, exceeding the commonly cited 300 mg/day threshold; saturated fat is low at 0.9 g/100g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 150–200 g to keep dietary cholesterol below 300 mg per meal, and should request minimal oil and reduced sodium seasoning to manage both lipid and blood pressure risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Tomato is a nightshade vegetable (solanine-containing) that may aggravate inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals; the dish also lacks meaningful omega-3 anti-inflammatory content.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor individual symptom response to tomato and chilli (nightshades), consider substituting with non-nightshade vegetables such as bok choy or zucchini, and reduce fish sauce to lower the sodium burden that may worsen tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Squid with Cucumber and Tomato (Vietnamese: Mực Xào Dưa Cà) is a Vietnamese home-style and restaurant dish in which cleaned squid rings or tubes are rapidly stir-fried over high heat with sliced cucumber and ripe tomato wedges, seasoned with fish sauce, soy sauce, garlic, and a small amount of sugar. The cooking method is stir-frying (xào), which preserves the tender, slightly chewy texture of the squid and the crispness of the cucumber while allowing the tomato to soften into a light, savoury sauce. The flavour profile is umami-forward with a mild sweetness from the tomato and a fresh, cooling note from the cucumber. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 108.6 kcal per 100 g, with 10.0 g protein from squid, 5.1 g fat (predominantly unsaturated), and only 5.1 g carbohydrate, making it a low-glycaemic, high-protein seafood dish. Sodium is elevated at 342.9 mg/100g due to fish sauce and soy sauce seasoning. Cholesterol is high at 128.6 mg/100g, characteristic of squid. The dish is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low carbohydrate and low GI (~35), but requires sodium monitoring for hypertension and portion control for cardiovascular disease due to squid cholesterol. It is dairy-free and halal-compatible when prepared with halal-certified fish sauce.
common_user_queries: Is squid with tomato good for diabetes?
How much sodium in Vietnamese stir-fried squid?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this Vietnamese composite dish exists in any primary approved regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values were cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central component data for squid, cucumber, tomato, fish sauce, and soy sauce, and are broadly consistent with the input record's values. Confidence is medium because the values are estimated from components rather than a direct measurement of the prepared dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this Vietnamese stir-fried squid composite dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved primary source as a prepared dish entry as of 2026-04-16. No zero-macro justification required as all core macros (protein_g 10.0, fat_g 5.1, carbohydrate_g 5.1) are non-zero. Input record data_source was listed as 'grabfood' (a forbidden source); values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central component data and retained where consistent. GI estimated at 35 (low) using analogous low-GI vegetable and seafood components from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (35 × 5.1 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 6.3 g.
